content:
{
  "title": "Adversarial Role Interleaving: A Prompting Technique for Robust LLM Output Control",
  "problem": "Large language models can be manipulated by adversarial or jailbreak prompts, leading to unauthorized or harmful outputs. Existing prompting strategies often fail to consistently mitigate these attacks.",
  "motivation": "Ensuring robust control over LLM outputs is critical as these models are increasingly deployed in safety-sensitive settings. Current adversarial attacks exploit context sensitivity, prompt injection, and instruction-following biases in LLMs. Prompting innovations that explicitly inoculate models against such manipulations are urgently needed.",
  "method": "We propose \"Adversarial Role Interleaving\" (ARI), a prompting method that dynamically alternates roles (e.g., user, system, assistant, adversary) within a single prompt sequence. Each prompt section includes contrasting instructions—both compliant and adversarial—encoded as explicit role statements. The model is then queried under a meta-instruction to consistently prioritize system-aligned outputs regardless of adversarial content. This approach harnesses LLMs' sensitivity to role and instruction framing, reducing susceptibility to jailbreaks by forcing the model to resolve conflicting role signals in favor of the system's directive.",
  "experiment_plan": "ARI will be evaluated on jailbreak/adversarial prompt benchmarks (e.g., AdvBench, JailbreakBench) using models such as GPT-4 and Llama-2. Baseline comparisons will include standard prompting, system message reinforcement, and chain-of-thought defenses. Metrics will include attack success rate, benign task performance, and output alignment as measured by human and automated evaluators."
}
